title: Code Splitting
intro: Your single-page app loads a 2MB JavaScript bundle before showing anything. It's like forcing someone to download an entire encyclopedia just to read one article. Users on slow connections wait 10+ seconds. The app has a dashboard, a settings page, an admin panel, and a chart library -- but most users only visit the dashboard. Time to split things up.

teach.title: Code Splitting = Chapters in a Book
teach.body: Imagine if you had to download an entire encyclopedia before you could read one article. That's what a single JavaScript bundle does -- it forces users to download code for every page, even pages they'll never visit. **Code splitting** is like splitting the encyclopedia into chapters that load on demand.

teach.title: Code Splitting = Grocery Delivery in Bags
teach.body: Imagine if your grocery delivery service sent you every single item from the store, even though you only ordered eggs and milk. You'd wait forever for the truck to arrive. **Code splitting** is like having the delivery service send only what you ordered now, and delivering the rest later if you add to your order. Users get what they need fast, without downloading the entire store.

teach.title: How Code Splitting Works
teach.body:
Instead of one giant JavaScript file, code splitting creates multiple smaller files:

1. **Route-based splitting** -- Each page becomes its own chunk (dashboard.js, settings.js, admin.js)
2. **Component-based splitting** -- Heavy components load only when rendered (chart library, rich text editor)
3. **Library splitting** -- Vendor code (React, lodash) goes in a separate chunk that caches well

The user only downloads the code needed for the page they're viewing.

teach.title: React.lazy for Route Splitting
teach.annotation: Each page component is wrapped in lazy(), so its JavaScript file only downloads when the user navigates to that route. Suspense shows a loading fallback while the chunk downloads.

teach.body: Code splitting is how apps built with AI go from hobby projects to professional products. When your dashboard loads in 2 seconds instead of 10, users stick around long enough to hit that subscribe button.

Code splitting breaks your JavaScript into smaller chunks that load on demand. Split by route (each page is a chunk), by component (heavy libraries load when needed), and by vendor (third-party code caches separately).

start.narrative:
Your app's single bundle is 2MB. You analyze it and find:

- Dashboard code: 200KB
- Settings page: 150KB
- Admin panel: 300KB
- Chart library (used only in admin): 600KB
- PDF generator (used only in settings): 400KB
- Shared React + utilities: 350KB

80% of users only visit the dashboard. What's your first code splitting strategy?

choices.text: Split by route -- each page becomes its own chunk
Split the chart library into its own chunk since it's the biggest
Minify everything harder to make the single bundle smaller

route-split.narrative:
You split the app into route-based chunks:

- Main chunk (shared code): 350KB
- Dashboard chunk: 200KB
- Settings + PDF generator: 550KB
- Admin + charts: 900KB

Now dashboard users only download 550KB instead of 2MB. The admin chunk is still 900KB because the chart library lives inside it.

choices.text: Lazy-load the chart library at the component level so it only loads when the charts component renders
Leave the admin chunk at 900KB -- only admins see it

chart-split.narrative: Splitting the chart library into its own chunk shaves 600KB off the initial download, but you're still sending 1.4MB to dashboard users who don't need settings, admin, or PDF code. Route-based splitting is the bigger lever for the first cut.

choices.text: Start with route-based splitting first, then come back to the chart library

minify-only.narrative: Your code is already minified. You might squeeze out another 5-10%, but the fundamental problem remains: you're sending 2MB of code to users who only need 200KB. The solution isn't smaller files -- it's fewer files loaded upfront.

choices.text: Try route-based splitting instead of just compression

preload-strategy.narrative: Splitting alone introduces a new problem: when a user clicks the link to the admin panel, they now wait while the chunk downloads on a cold cache. The Suspense fallback flashes. The page feels slower than before, even though the bundle is smaller. How do you bridge that gap?

choices.text: Preload the admin and settings chunks on hover/focus of their nav links so the chunk is in cache by the time the user clicks
Prefetch all secondary chunks during browser idle time after the dashboard hydrates
Add a longer Suspense fallback skeleton so users don't notice the wait
Skip preloading -- the on-demand wait is acceptable

measure-impact.narrative: Splits and preloads are wired up. Before you ship, you need to confirm the change actually helped (and didn't accidentally hurt) something measurable. What's your verification step?

choices.text: Run Lighthouse on dashboard, settings, and admin pages on a throttled 'Slow 4G' profile and compare LCP/TTI before vs after
Check the production bundle analyzer to confirm chunk sizes match expectations
Reload the dashboard once locally on a fast connection and call it good
Skip measurement -- smaller initial chunk has to be faster

ship-iterate.narrative: Numbers look good in synthetic tests. But the real users you care about are on a mix of devices and networks you can't simulate. How do you ship the change in a way that catches a regression you didn't anticipate?

choices.text: Ship behind a 50/50 rollout with real-user monitoring (Web Vitals to your analytics) and roll back if p75 LCP regresses
Ship to 100% of users and watch the Web Vitals dashboard for the next 24 hours
Ship to 100% of users and trust the synthetic Lighthouse numbers

end-good.narrative: Real-user p75 LCP drops from 6.8s to 2.4s for dashboard users on slow connections. Admin-page TTI is unchanged (preload absorbs the cold-cache cost). The 50/50 rollout caught a regression in Safari that you fixed before going to 100%. Dashboard load time on Slow 4G dropped from 10 seconds to under 3 seconds.
end-good.feedback: End-to-end performance work done right. You picked the highest-leverage split first (routes), pushed deeper (component-level for the chart library), bridged the cold-cache gap with preloading, verified with synthetic tests, and shipped behind a guarded rollout backed by real-user metrics. That's the loop that turns an optimization from a guess into a measured win.

end-okay.narrative: The change goes out to everyone at once. Most users see the improvement, but a subset on a specific device class regresses and you only notice three days later when your analytics weekly review surfaces it. You roll forward a fix, but slow users had a worse week than they needed to.
end-okay.feedback: Synthetic tests + post-launch monitoring is much better than nothing, but it puts the entire user base on the experimental version at once. A staged rollout (10% → 50% → 100%) with automated rollback on a Web Vitals regression catches device- or network-specific regressions before they hit everyone.

end-bad.narrative: Lighthouse said it was faster, so you shipped to 100%. A week later you notice bounce rate up on settings -- preloading is fighting the dashboard's own data fetch on slow connections, and you've made the page worse for the users you most wanted to help. You roll back.
end-bad.feedback: Lighthouse is a synthetic single-run on one device profile -- it tells you what could happen, not what is happening. Real-user monitoring (RUM) is what tells you whether your change actually improved p75 LCP for the users you have. Always pair synthetic measurement with a guarded rollout backed by RUM.
